summary_sections.title: Lesson Overview
summary_sections.content_html:
Overview

This lesson covers the fundamental measures of center used to analyze and summarize data sets. You'll learn how to calculate and apply the mean, median, and mode, and understand when each is most appropriate.

summary_sections.title: Definitions and Concepts
summary_sections.content_html:
Key Definitions

What is the definition of a 'physical property'?

Physical properties (color, density, melting point, hardness, conductivity, luster, solubility) describe the substance as it is — no new substance is formed during observation. The substance retains its chemical identity.

What is the definition of a 'chemical property'?

Chemical properties describe what a substance CAN become — they can only be observed when the substance undergoes a chemical change. Examples: flammability, reactivity, oxidation potential, toxicity.

A jeweler heats a metal sample to test it. The metal melts at 1,064°C, is shiny yellow, very malleable, and does not corrode or tarnish. She then dips it in strong acid — no reaction occurs. Which properties are physical, which are chemical, and what is the metal?

Melting point, color, and malleability are measured without creating new substances — physical properties. Resistance to corrosion and acid tests involve whether the substance reacts — chemical properties. Gold has all these characteristics: MP 1,064°C, yellow, malleable, and extremely low reactivity.

summary_sections.title: Real-World Applications
summary_sections.content_html:
Real-World Applications

Example: An art restorer must identify whether a painting frame is solid gold or gold-plated copper. She cannot cut or damage the frame. Which combination of physical and chemical property tests could help without destruction?

Density measurement (physical property) can reveal if the object is too light for pure gold (19.3 g/cm³ vs. copper's 8.96). An acid test on a tiny hidden spot (chemical property) is definitive: nitric acid dissolves copper (turns green) but does not react with gold. Both methods are minimally destructive.
